cataloging_subject.label: The Legend of Theophilus (Northern Homily Cycle)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The single anonymous Middle English verse exemplum about Theophilus as transmitted within the Northern Homily Cycle and represented by CME00065. It is not the complete Northern Homily Cycle, Kölbing's entire 1877 German article, either physical manuscript, a two-part work, the U-M encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact. Kölbing's H and V texts are parallel witness representations of one work.

catalog_summary.title: The Legend of Theophilus (Northern Homily Cycle)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Northern Homily Cycle poet
catalog_summary.date: Probably around 1300 or the early fourteenth century; earliest surviving exact witness identified here dated 1340-1360; first published in 1877
catalog_summary.region: Northern England, perhaps Yorkshire; surviving copies span northern, Midland, southern, and Irish transmission

titles.value: The Legend of Theophilus (Northern Homily Cycle)
titles.supplied_by: General Corpus, following DIMEV's work identification while distinguishing this version from other Theophilus legends

titles.value: The Legend of Theophilus in the Northern Homily Cycle
A bishop woned beyond the sea / In Sicily the rich city
notes: Witness spellings and the second-line syntax vary; this is a discovery form rather than a diplomatic quotation from every copy.

titles.value: Die jüngere englische Fassung der Theophilussage

scope.description: Title of the 1877 edition representing the work, not a medieval witness title

agents.name: Anonymous Northern Homily Cycle poet
notes: The singular label is conventional. The author's identity is unknown; a probable Austin canon is a scholarly hypothesis, not a named attribution.
Do not assign the work to Richard Rolle: Kölbing says resemblance to Prick of Conscience does not establish the same author.

scope.description: Complete H and V witness streams
Selective comparison of the closely related T witness against H

notes: T is represented by corrections and selected variants, not by a third continuous stream.

date_statements.display: Probably around 1300 or in the early fourteenth century; the cycle-wide 1295-1306 proposal is not definitive for this item

assertion.notes: The range conservatively represents current cycle-level dating and should not be mistaken for a directly dated authorial copy.

date_statements.display: 1340-1360, in Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Ashmole 42

assertion.notes: Earliest surviving exact witness identified in the present fourteen-witness census, not necessarily the date of composition.

notes: Kölbing's title explicitly describes the version as published for the first time.
Kölbing argues from rhyme that the poem is northern and that the Vernon scribe southernized forms.

assertion.notes: Yorkshire is a cycle-wide scholarly possibility, not a securely localized composition place for this item.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: German exposition and Latin rubrics belong to Kölbing's edition or witness paratext, not to the abstract work's primary language statement.

form_statements.description: Octosyllabic rhyming couplets; DIMEV describes the versification as two-line aa.

genre_statements.label: Sermon exempla
notes: The tale is embedded in the Northern Homily Cycle's thirteenth-Sunday-after-Trinity homily in H.

subject_statements.label: Clerical ambition and a pact with the devil
Anti-Jewish sorcerer stereotype
notes: The tale uses a Jewish magician as the intermediary in Theophilus's pact with the devil. This subject heading records harmful narrative content rather than endorsing it.

summaries.value: Theophilus, a virtuous episcopal official, refuses elevation and later loses his office. Driven by wounded status and despair, he seeks a Jewish magician, renounces Christ and the Virgin, and signs a pact with the devil. Repenting, he undertakes penance and prays to Mary, who intercedes for him and restores the written pact. After public confession and communion, he dies reconciled. The Northern Homily Cycle uses the narrative as an exemplum of pride, sin, penance, confession, Marian intercession, mercy, and salvation.

CME00065 serializes Kölbing's H stream and V stream as two sequential divisions because XML cannot reproduce the printed parallel columns directly. H has 798 numbered lines; V has 725 printed line numbers. These are manuscript-witness representations of one tale and must not be combined into an intrinsic 1,523- or 1,524-line work extent.

Kölbing knew three copies in 1877 and directly used H, T, and V. DIMEV now lists fourteen witnesses. The eleven additional copies are collateral modern-census evidence, not retroactive sources of Kölbing's edition. Kölbing argues that H/T and V descend through divergent classes from a common source but does not claim that either derives directly from the other or that the common source can be reconstructed.

extent.description: One verse exemplum. No single intrinsic line count is asserted because Kölbing and CME00065 present two divergent complete witness streams: H has 798 numbered lines; V has 725 numbered lines. The XML contains 1,524 L elements (798 H plus 726 V elements), one more V element than the printed V numbering because an encoding position offset appears around the N=550 milestone. The XML's 56 LG groups are also representation-level, not a stable abstract-work stanza count.

content_parts.label: H witness stream (Harley MS 4196)
content_parts.summary: Kölbing's complete H text, selectively corrected and annotated from the closely related, fire-damaged T witness.
notes: Representation-only part: it is not an intrinsic chapter or a separately authored work.
T at ff. 221rb-225rb contributes selected variants and corrections but not another continuous encoded stream.

content_parts.label: V witness stream (Vernon Manuscript)
content_parts.summary: Kölbing's complete V text, preserving a substantially divergent recension that the editor prints parallel to H.

locators.value: second DIV2, V stream; 726 L elements

notes: Representation-only part: it is not an intrinsic chapter or a separately authored work.
The metrics report XML elements. Printed V numbering ends at 725; the extra XML L element reflects an encoding/numbering offset and is not evidence of a 726-line authorial text.

related_works.title: The Northern Homily Cycle
notes: In H the tale concludes the homily for the thirteenth Sunday after Trinity. The abstract tale and complete cycle remain distinct cataloging units.

evidence.summary: Direct evidence for artifact identifiers, language attribute, two sequential H/V divisions, headings, exact element counts, printed milestones, notes, and revision history.

evidence.citation: Eugen Kölbing, 'Die jüngere englische Fassung der Theophilussage,' Englische Studien 1 (1877): 16-57; Nachtrag 186; corrections 540.
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for title, plot, first publication, H/V/T witnesses and method, line counts, couplet form, H/V textual relationship, northern-origin argument, editorial qualifications, and edition boundaries.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, record 10 (IMEV 25; NIMEV 25).
evidence.summary: Current work-level authority for preferred identification, first line, two-line aa form, subjects, manuscript titles, fourteen-witness census, exact work loci, and editions.

evidence.citation: Anne B. Thompson, ed., The Northern Homily Cycle (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2008), introduction and manuscript descriptions.
evidence.summary: Specialist evidence for cycle identity, anonymous authorship, probable around-1300 or early-fourteenth-century date, northern or perhaps Yorkshire origin, verse-sermon context, and bounded Austin-canon and dating hypotheses. Its edited text omits this item.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian, MS Eng. poet. a. 1, complete IIIF object, canvases 294-297.
evidence.summary: Complete-object facsimile evidence for the V witness title, exact ff. 203vc-205rc locus, column boundaries, and material manuscript context.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 4196.
evidence.summary: Official witness evidence for current shelfmark, date, material, Northern Homily Cycle extent, and the physical codex containing H.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries Medieval Manuscripts, MS Ashmole 42.
evidence.summary: Official catalog evidence for the 1340-1360 date of the earliest surviving exact witness identified in the present census.

evidence.citation: Structural inspection of CME00065.xml and comparison of L positions with printed N milestones.
evidence.summary: Confirms 798 H L elements, 726 V L elements, 16 H and 40 V LG elements, and a one-element V position offset around N=550 before printed numbering resumes through 725.

open_questions.question: Can this Theophilus item's composition date be narrowed independently of cycle-wide proposals?
next_steps: Review post-2008 scholarship on the Northern Homily Cycle and the Carver 1295-1306 proposal, recording competing ranges separately.

open_questions.question: Does later scholarship materially strengthen or weaken the proposed Austin-canon authorship context for this item?
next_steps: Treat the cycle poet as anonymous unless a directly supported attribution emerges; do not convert institutional milieu into a named author.

open_questions.question: Should an abstract-work line count ever be asserted across the divergent H and V recensions, and how should the one-element V XML numbering offset be corrected?
next_steps: Collate the V XML against printed pp. 38-57 and Vernon canvases 294-297, then correct the encoding only in a separately authorized change; retain witness-specific counts in the meantime.

notes: The preferred English title is supplied for discovery and disambiguation; witnesses preserve several different rubrics and some none.
The DIMEV witness census is current collateral evidence. Only H, T, and V are documented inputs to Kölbing's 1877 edition.
German and Latin are edition or paratext languages and therefore are not included in the abstract work's language_codes.
The anti-Jewish stereotype subject note is included to support responsible discovery and contextualization of harmful narrative content.
Whole-manuscript facsimile availability, exact target mappings, cache state, and image-use rights belong to the linked lineage manifest rather than being collapsed into descriptive work metadata.
